chapters.title: Great leaders enjoy sharing their thoughts and ideas with the world in a captivating way
chapters.text:
Almost no other book on the market today delves further into the science of communication than Talk Like TED. It exposes you to men and women who prepare and give the talk of their life, including scientists, writers, educators, environmentalists, and well-known leaders.

When it comes to expressing their thoughts, some individuals are naturally gifted. As a result of their abilities, they have gained more status and influence in contemporary society. When a daring concept is presented by an outstanding speaker, there is nothing more motivating than that! Ideas, if packaged and presented properly, have the potential to alter the course of human history. So, wouldn't it be incredible to learn the precise methods used by the world's most effective communicators, see them give extremely inspiring presentations, and then use their secrets to amaze your audiences yourself?

This tidbit includes a proper analysis of hundreds of TED presentations, direct interviews with TED's most popular speakers, and Carmine Gallo's personal insights taken from years of coaching inspiring leaders of the world's most admired organizations. This bite-sized book is for everyone who wishes to communicate with more confidence and power in their own voice. Those who give presentations, sell goods and services, or lead groups of individuals who need to be motivated would benefit from this course. If you have valuable ideas to share, the methods in this summary will assist you in crafting and delivering those ideas much more convincingly than you could have anticipated.

chapters.title: You can unleash the master within you by understanding what gives you passion
chapters.text:
Mastery is defined by how you express your passion, and passion provides the road to mastery. Aimee Mullins' passion was seeing herself at her full potential, despite her handicap. Her legs had to be amputated below the knees due to a medical issue she was born with. She refused to accept the label “disabled,” as she grew up, which is why she sought out prosthetic legs to help her walk.

Enthusiasm is infectious, and you can't inspire people until you're inspired yourself, since it's easier to pass on inspiration when your own passion is visible.

Passion isn't always derived from what brings you recognition or attention. Mullins was a Paralympic track and field athlete, a fashion model, and an actor, but it was speaking about human potential that sparked her enthusiasm.

Passion may stem from a variety of sources. People who are experts in a variety of areas are constantly passionate about what makes their hearts sing. It wasn't about the coffee for Howard Schultz, the creator of Starbucks, but about creating a third space between work and home, a place where workers would be treated with respect and provided with excellent services. Similarly, Tony Hsieh, the founder of Zappos, was more concerned in providing pleasure than in selling shoes. You get greater confidence by sharing your motivation with the audience.

You cannot have a great job if you are not enthusiastic about what you do or if your work does not bring you joy. Passion provides you a motivating mindset that helps you achieve your goals while persuading others to do the same. The majority of passionate leaders are public speakers who like sharing their thoughts and ideas with the rest of the world.

You'll be well on your way to releasing the master inside after you figure out what makes your heart sing and how to communicate your tales to an audience.

Did you know? According to Wikipedia, over a million people watch TED talks online.

chapters.title: You stand a better chance at making a great presentation when you master the art of storytelling
chapters.text:
The thoughts and emotions of listeners are captured by good storytellers. When civil rights attorney Bryan Stevenson gave a TED lecture in 2011, the audience listened because he had captivated their hearts and minds.

Scientific studies show that good tales excite the human brain, resulting in a deep bond between the speaker and the audience.

People readily agree on identification, and they feel more engaged with a narrative when they can identify it. You may easily contact souls and get your words through with little or no opposition if you create an identity around your tales. Persuasion is best accomplished via storytelling.

Aristotle, a Greek philosopher, argues that persuasion happens when three elements are present:

Ethos. Ethos is concerned with the information's trustworthiness. Humans are more likely to agree with individuals whose accomplishments and experience they admire.

Logos. Logos work with facts and data as persuasive weapons.

Pathos. Pathos is the act of appealing to human emotions.

Stevenson was a superb storyteller because he had Pathos. He holds the record for receiving the most standing ovations during a TED presentation.

Everyone has a tale to tell. Everyone has a story to tell, and everyone is telling a story. There are three different kinds of stories:

A well-crafted and well-told narrative provides a significant advantage to a leader. Successful commercials are usually built around a narrative; jingles and adverts are built on storylines that are intended to pique the audience's interest.

Stories may help create a strong brand and drive your company to success if you're a business-minded and creative person. Stories are more effective at illuminating and illustrating concepts and tactics than PowerPoint or file presentations. Stories sell ideas better, which is why, if you want to thrive in your profession, you should be a great storyteller.

chapters.title: Continuous practices and rehearsals are effective ways to efficiently prepare a great presentation
chapters.text:
Consistent rehearsals, preparation, and hard effort are required for good presentations. Practice your topic until you can give it as naturally as if you were talking to a friend. Stories must be conveyed via gestures and body language in addition to words. Your audience will lose their unique connection to you if your gestures are shoddy and confusing, and you will fail to create a significant impression.

Amanda Palmer, a performance artist and musician, described the process of putting together a brilliant speech by saying she slaved over it, writing, writing, writing, re-writing, timing, re-timing, adjusting, and attempting to squeeze precise pieces of information into 12 short minutes.

Practice allows you to plan well and ahead of time. It enables you to stick to the time limit without boring your audience with lengthy, incomprehensible remarks.

You'll be easily sidetracked if you don't rehearse a conversation since you'll be thinking about a lot of stuff instead of concentrating on your primary point.

Great conversations or presentations take you to ideas you’d never considered. ~ Carmine Gallo

Palmer devised three stages for rehearsing a scenario in which she would deliver her speech. They are as follows:

Assistance with planning. This stage involves obtaining information from a variety of reliable sources. There is never too much information, and no one is an island of knowledge. Palmer collected information from a variety of sources, as well as the condition in her immediate surroundings.

Feedback from the start. It's a good method to gauge how intriguing your narrative is if it's subject to scrutiny and critique. It's the same reason why a film is shown to a small number of people before being shown to a larger public. Palmer was able to see her flaws and work on them by exposing herself to her former theater director and high school mentor.

Continual rehearsal. Continuous rehearsal is a kind of reinforcement that helps you remember information and ideas better. When you practice regularly, there's a good possibility you'll become better at something.

As a speaker, your true power comes from inside. It boils down to how effectively and thoroughly you deliver your speeches, presentations, or tales. When you practice often, your motions and delivery will improve.

chapters.title: Novelty is a great way to create a strong connection with your audience
chapters.text:
Humans are fascinated by new experiences. The human brain enjoys learning something new, something that intrigues the listener's intellect and spirit. Listeners' attention is drawn to new ideas and discoveries, which puts their brains in a state of readiness to absorb information about these discoveries.

Humans are natural explorers, with an inherent drive to learn more about things that aren't immediately apparent. The human mind craves more information, which is why excellent tales and presentations must be rich in facts and findings.

You can only learn more if you expand your knowledge, particularly if you expand your knowledge outside your area.

According to Martha Burns, an associate professor at Northwestern, when the brain gets enthusiastic about a reward, it produces a liquid substance called “dopamine.” Dopamine is the chemical that makes you happy when you win the lottery, make a good wager, or use drugs. When you acquire new knowledge, your brain experiences the same response. You may dramatically boost your dopamine levels, as well as those of your audience, by simply making knowledge fresh and interesting.

You have to really be courageous about your instincts and your ideas. Otherwise you’ll just knuckle under, and things that might have been memorable will be lost. ~ Francis Ford

When you educate your audience about something they didn't know before, you may make the difference between a dull speech and a fantastic one. There is data in other areas that would further illustrate your argument. Plato and Aristotle, for example, were experts in a variety of disciplines. Making a good presentation encourages you to come up with new ideas, which allows you to keep learning.

When you disclose a fresh concept or discovery to your audience, you're demonstrating to them that you really understand what you're talking about and that you're aware of new methods to address issues. By doing so, you will have obtained access to their soul, where they will be waiting for you to speak to their thoughts.

chapters.title: Great presentations are remembered by memorable events
chapters.text:
Bill Gates, the founder of Microsoft, wanted to give a presentation to many global leaders on Malaria and how it might be controlled in Africa and Asia. He provided a spectacular moment when he pulled out a jar full of mosquitoes and then unleashed them in the conference room to make the presentation exciting. When Gates announced that the mosquitoes were Malaria-free, the crowd was shocked and puzzled, yet comfortable. Gates' “mosquitoes moment” was the most remembered and talked about part of his TED address, which lasted 18 minutes. Several news sources and wealthy social media accounts centered their coverage on the event, causing the story to spread quickly across the globe.

A successful presentation or speaker always provides the audience with at least one jaw-dropping moment that makes the whole event stay in their minds.

When you create a memorable moment in a presentation, you're generating an “emotionally charged event” or emotional competent stimulus (ECS) as scientists put it.

Because it is a memory that may be remembered at any moment, the ECS is the hook that sticks in your audience's heads. Shock, pleasure, happiness, sorrow, fear, surprise, and other emotions are dealt with by the ECS. When you offer these moments, you grab your audience's attention while also leaving them with a lasting recollection.

In another example, the audience were visibly horrified by the gruesome image before them as Dr. Jill, a neuroanatomist, gave a graphical presentation on the human brain. Despite their revulsion, they were completely absorbed in the presentation and attentive to what was going on. The brain is wired to remember emotionally charged and vivid experiences more than mundane activities.

Deliver something unique and emotionally intense to make a memorable experience. You should also try to finish your presentation on a high note, rather than letting your pace sink.

chapters.title: Humor is a tool that works well for great speakers
chapters.text:
Author Sir Ken Robinson gave the world's most-watched and popular TED talk, which lasted 18 minutes and included a lot of comedy. Robinson discussed how American schooling discourages risk-taking and kills innovation. Robinson's speech, which has had over 25 million views, is renowned for combining innovation and comedy to make a powerful message.

Humor provides relaxation, which brings pleasure or peace. However, only be humorous when required.

There's a distinction between incorporating comedy into your presentation and telling jokes to the audience. Your presentation will most likely become dull and lifeless as a result of the jokes.

There are five ways in which you can be funny without telling jokes. They are:

Personal tales, observations, and anecdotes. When you tell tales from your own life and experiences, they tend to be humorous. This isn't to suggest that you should talk about your personal problems in front of your audience.

Metaphors and analogies. You may generate a funny mood among your audience by comparing points and analyzing parallels between events and information. Make a funny comparison out of two or more events, experiences, or individuals that isn't rude or inappropriate.

Quotes. To your audience, quote a humorous remark made by someone else. Allow your quotations to have a connection to your first presentation. Quoting someone or anything unrelated to your subject may throw the viewers off.

Video. Video demonstration relieves the stress of having to be humorous, and it enables you to slip in one or two presentations that would otherwise be dull. Funny films may elicit heartfelt laughter, allowing your audience to relax and open up to you.

Photos. Funny images, like videos, put you at ease since the audience has warmed up to your pleasant demeanor. Use images that relate to your topic while also eliciting a laugh from the audience.

Humor involves courage and absolute carefulness. Great presenters know when and when not to insert humor in a presentation, and you should too.

chapters.title: Avoid unnecessarily long speeches and stick to the 18-minute rule
chapters.text:
The TED talk must not exceed 18 minutes, regardless of whether it is given by Bill Gates or Steve Jobs, and this is a guideline you should observe. The audience would most likely be bored by long presentations and speeches. A presentation should last no more than 18 minutes. If your presentation is going to be longer than 18 minutes, you'll need to take pauses in between. Long presentations detract from the topic's reason, which is why 18 minutes is excellent. Researchers discovered that having too much information (cognitive backlog) inhibits thoughts from being sent successfully.

Cognitive thinking, according to Texas University's Dr. Paul King, is a depleting activity that affects both the speaker and the listeners. Listening is exhausting, and too much of it completely obliterates the information being conveyed.

The harder the listener has to work to comprehend the information being presented, the longer the presentation will take.

Limitations encourage creative thinking and activity, particularly time constraints. When you have a time limit, you're forced to go through your presentation for the most essential points and attempt to condense them within 18 minutes.

To condense huge concepts into an 18-minute presentation, you'll need intelligence, which is where the Rule of Three comes in. The rule of three, often known as the three A's of Awesome, was created by award-winning blogger Neil Pasricha.

Attitude. The way you handle a situation, particularly a negative one, is determined by your attitude. You can either work on it for a better tomorrow or you can wallow in your failure and become trapped in the past.

Awareness. When you revert to your younger self, you'll see things you've seen before in a new light.

Authenticity. Being true to yourself, your goals, and your individuality is what authenticity is all about.

chapters.title: Create a vivid mental picture by appealing to multisensory experiences
chapters.text:
Because the human brain does not pay attention to dull things, you must appeal to more than one of the five senses in your presentation.

When you combine images, music, and emotion in a presentation, your audience will be attracted to you because they can see and feel what you're seeing and experiencing.

At a TED presentation, Michael Pritchard showed his LIFESAVER water and filtration system to the audience. He began by showing them a photo of a child drinking contaminated water before asking them whether they knew where the water they were drinking originated from. He showed how to filter dirty water into clean water in seconds at the conclusion of his presentation, no matter how bad the water was. Without giving a lengthy speech, this presentation consisted of examples, data, and images that conveyed his narrative.

Simple graphics and illustrations breathe life into your presentation, helping you to build a brand and engage your audience.

It is simple to communicate a message by appealing to the sensory elements of the human brain. You don't need to read a long handbook to figure out what the voltage symbol on electrical wires or high tension lines means and why touching them is dangerous.

Keeping your audience engaged needs a feeling of belonging, and the best way to do it is to appeal to their many senses. Making such presentations requires bravery, but you must be bold if you want to give a great TED talk.

Your ideas must astound and wow your audience. Allow them to admire your brilliance and inventiveness for a minute. That way, you may have an influence on their life and alter the world as you want.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
TED talks are detailed summaries of various ideas and strategies across various fields of study. To be a great TED talker, you must forget about being average, and focus on being a professional. Your job is to inspire and influence people with your presentation and speeches, not just to deliver a presentation.

There are numerous benefits to remaining loyal to oneself. Your audience would comprise some of the world's most brilliant minds and brains, making it one of the worst locations in which to pretend to be someone you are not. Almost everyone in your audience will be able to tell if anything is a hoax or a fake. Pretense will not serve you well, and it will only lead to a loss of public confidence in you.

Be truthful, transparent, and genuine in your communication. You can outsource and even replicate other people's ways, but unless you make a lasting effect on the world, you'll never be remembered for anything. Your mark serves as a visual representation of your identity that others can associate with you. People will not consider you amazing unless they notice a large number of things they can outsource and copy from you as well. If you want to motivate your audience, you should make them look forward to hearing from you in the next few minutes.

Place a considerable effort into making your presentation stand out, and you'll find it simple to persuade the audience as to why they should pay attention to you.

Try this

Time yourself, and do all you can to deliver a great presentation in 18 minutes. Keep doing it until you can do it in less time.

Read it till the end?
